symptom: Vibrating, walking, or making abnormal noise after balancing

steps.do: Stop the run immediately and wait for the rotor to fully stop before opening the lid.
steps.why: Unexpected vibration can indicate imbalance, cracked tubes, rotor seating problems, or mechanical failure.

steps.do: Confirm tubes are opposite each other with matching mass, fill level, tube type, caps, and adapters.
steps.why: Visual balance is not enough when density, caps, or adapters differ.

steps.do: Inspect tubes, buckets, adapters, and rotor wells for cracks, leaks, debris, or missing cushions.
steps.why: Small defects can create imbalance or aerosol risk.

steps.do: Reseat the rotor or buckets according to the equipment manual if trained to do so.
steps.why: A partially seated rotor can vibrate even with correctly paired samples.

steps.do: Restart at the lowest appropriate speed only if inspection is clean and the manual allows retry.
steps.why: A low-speed check reduces risk before returning to the protocol speed.

steps.do: If vibration repeats, power down, label the unit out of service, preserve the sample state, and call the equipment owner.
steps.why: Repeated vibration can signal a rotor or drive fault that field troubleshooting should not override.

escalate_if: Any tube breakage, leak, smell, visible rotor damage, failed latch, repeated vibration, or unknown sample hazard -> stop work, isolate if safe, and contact the supervisor/equipment owner.
